FT_IC1.evidence: Cohort study using ABCD Study release 5.1, longitudinal cohort of participants aged 9-10 at baseline, 21 US recruitment sites (2016-2022).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using latent class linear mixed models to characterize addictive use trajectories and Poisson regression models to estimate associations with suicidal behaviors and mental health outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original investigation published in JAMA; no indication of duplicate or preprint.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) and MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire) are qualifying SM/phone-specific variables used as exposures (IVs). Addictive use trajectories of social media and mobile phones predict suicidal behaviors (e.g., increasing SMAQ trajectory: RR=2.14, 95% CI 1.61-2.85), suicidal ideation (RR=1.46), and CBCL internalizing/externalizing symptoms (mean difference=1.27) — all individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numerous numeric results reported: risk ratios with 95% CIs from Poisson regression (e.g., RR=2.39 for high-peaking SMAQ trajectory and suicidal behaviors) and mean differences for CBCL T-scores across trajectory groups.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses with quantitative results present throughout (Figure 3, Figure 4, Table); FDR-corrected p-values reported for all main estimates.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone (SMAQ, MPIQ, VGAQ) are used as IVs (exposures in latent trajectory models predicting suicidal behaviors and mental health outcomes at year 4 follow-up), not as DVs. EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis; here SM is IV throughout.
notes: Paper also includes VGAQ (video game addictive use), which on its own would not qualify under IC2 binding clarification 2, but the qualifying SM (SMAQ) and phone (MPIQ) variables independently satisfy IC2 Step 1. Total screen time included as a separate non-qualifying covariate analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study longitudinal dataset, nearly 12,000 participants aged 9-10 at baseline from 21 US sites; final sample n=543 with year 2 and year 4 imaging data.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear mixed models (LMMs) to examine VGA x Time and SMA x Time interactions on fMRI beta-weights from MID task across two longitudinal waves (year 2 and year 4 follow-up).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Computers in Human Behavior; no indication of duplicate or preprint.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SMA (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire, SMAQ) is a qualifying SM-specific variable. SMA is included as an IV in every LMM predicting neural beta-weights (fMRI reward/loss sensitivity in nucleus accumbens, insula, ACC, amygdala, etc.) — individual-trait neural outcomes. SMA x Time interaction coefficients are reported in Table 3 for all 16 ROIs (e.g., SMA x Time on right insula loss contrast: b=-0.081, 95% CI [-0.15, -0.02], p=.017). The other side (fMRI beta-weights from MID task) is an individual-trait neural variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Standardized beta coefficients, 95% CIs, uncorrected and FDR-corrected p-values reported in Table 3 (SMA x Time on neural outcomes) and Table 4 (neural sensitivity x Time on VGA/SMA). Pearson r correlations in Table 2.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential LMM analyses with quantitative numeric results (b, SE, 95% CI, p, pFDR) reported for SMA and VGA effects on neural reward/loss sensitivity.

FT_EC7_reason: SMA appears as IV in hypothesis 1 models (predicting neural outcomes) and as DV in hypothesis 2 models (predicted by neural beta-weights x Time interactions). This is a bidirectional design where SM is both IV and DV across models; EC7 explicitly does not apply to bidirectional designs.
notes: Primary finding for SMA: no significant VGA/SMA x Time interaction effects on neural reward or loss sensitivity for SMA (all pFDR > 0.05). VGA shows significant bidirectional effects with neural loss sensitivity. SMA qualifies IC2 as the SM-specific IV; VGA alone (VGAQ) would not independently qualify under binding clarification 2, but SM qualification is sufficient.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cohort data from ABCD Study Release 5.1; participants at 2-year follow-up (T2, ages 11-12, n=9,054) and 4-year follow-up (T4, ages 13-14, n=4,007); multisite US longitudinal study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using Gaussian graphical models for cross-sectional networks (T2 and T4) and cross-lagged panel network (CLPN) analyses (T2 to T4) to examine centrality of problematic screen use indicators relative to screen time measures.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed full-length report in Journal of Behavioral Addictions; no indication of duplicate or preprint.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SMAQ (Problematic Social Media, node A2) and MPIQ (Problematic Mobile Phone, node A3) are qualifying SM/phone-specific variables. In CLPN models, A2 and A3 appear as IVs with cross-lagged edges to individual-trait outcomes: e.g., A2 (problematic social media) predicts somatic complaints C3 (B=0.302), behavioral activation BAS (B=0.179), delinquent peer involvement E3 (B=0.108), thought problems C5 (B=-0.251); A3 (problematic mobile phone) predicts impulsivity UPPS (B=0.219), behavioral activation BAS (B=0.194), behavioral inhibition BIS (B=0.108). Cross-sectional network edge weights also reported for SM/phone nodes connected to psychopathology and behavioral tendency nodes (individual-trait outcomes).

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported: CLPN edge coefficients (B values) in text and Figure 3; network centrality indices (strength, EI, bridge strength) with 95% CIs from bootstrapping in Figure 2 and Figure 4; e.g., problematic social media vs. social media time strength difference (T2 Female: 0.89 vs. 0.64, 95% CI=[0.141, 0.353]).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential CLPN and cross-sectional network analyses with quantitative edge weights and bootstrapped 95% CIs reported throughout results section.

FT_EC7_reason: The CLPN is a bidirectional cross-lagged design: SM/phone nodes (A2, A3) predict outcomes at T4 (SM as IV) AND are predicted by T2 nodes (SM as DV). Additionally, cross-sectional networks at T2 and T4 include SM/phone nodes as DVs (receiving edges from behavioral tendency and psychopathology nodes). EC7 explicitly does not apply to bidirectional/cross-lagged designs, and the cross-sectional carve-out also applies.
notes: Study includes VGAQ (video game time A1, problematic video game A1) and TV time alongside SM/phone measures. The SM (SMAQ) and phone (MPIQ) nodes independently satisfy IC2. The screen time measures (B1 video game time, B2 social media time) are generic activity-duration variables but the problematic use scales (SMAQ, MPIQ) are the qualifying variables.
